candidate_text: The first reaction was not a single public mood. It was a set of local control problems. On December 5, 2022, Meta Stack Overflow posted a temporary policy against ChatGPT-generated posts, saying the issue was not merely that answers could be wrong but that plausible wrong answers could arrive faster than volunteer moderators could inspect them. A month later, Chalkbeat reported that New York City's education department had blocked ChatGPT on school devices and networks, citing learning, safety, and accuracy concerns. Seattle Public Schools had taken a similar access-control route on district WiFi and devices, according to Axios Seattle, while Axios later reported that JPMorgan Chase restricted staff use under ordinary third-party-software controls rather than a named incident. These were not proof that developers, schools, or companies all rejected ChatGPT. They were early signs that the chat box had escaped the product category OpenAI had given it: communities had to decide whether it was a tool, a shortcut, a cheating machine, a security risk, or all of those at once.
allowed_claims: May support one scoped narrative paragraph joining Stack Overflow as a developer-community moderation example, NYC and Seattle as named education access-control examples, and JPMorgan as one workplace software-control example.
prohibited_claims: Do not write public panic, all developers, all schools, national school ban, broad company ban, boardroom panic, permanent bans, specific JPMorgan incident, or universal rejection.
